* **9.** *    * **In the six months ended September 30, 2025, and in Fiscals 2025, 2024 and 2023, we served 7,773, 8,585, 9,344** * * **and 6,935 children, respectively. Failure to retain existing clients, attract new clients, or maintain relationships** * * **with referral sources may adversely impact our revenues and growth** *
Our revenues are significantly dependent on our ability to maintain relationships with existing clients, attract new clients to our programmes, and maintain relationships with referral sources including stakeholders in the communities we serve. In the six months ended September 30, 2025, and in Fiscals 2025, 2024, and 2023, we served 7,773, 8,585, 9,344 and 6,935 children, respectively. A decline in either client retention, new enrolments, or referrals from our existing network can lead to underutilization of our centres, erosion of margins, and reduced profitability. Our referral sources are not, and cannot be, obligated to refer clients to us, and may instead direct them to other service providers. Our growth and profitability therefore depend, in part, on our ability to establish and maintain close working relationships with these referral sources, comply with applicable laws and contractual arrangements with respect to such relationships, and increase awareness and acceptance of the benefits of our services.

Client and referral source decisions to continue engaging with us are influenced by several factors, including satisfaction with results, the perceived value of our services, affordability, the eligible age limit for our programmes (children up to 15 years), and our reputation in the market. Many of our programmes require sustained family commitment, regular attendance, and adherence to therapy plans, which are beyond our direct control. If clients or referral sources are dissatisfied with our services, if we fail to provide or maintain a reputation for quality, or if our reputation is impacted by negative publicity, our enrolments could decline.
A sustained drop in enrolments or referrals at specific locations could result in further closures, adversely impacting our operational footprint, revenues, and growth prospects.

Further, our Company has had negative cash flows in the past three fiscals. Our Company had negative cash flow from “Operating activities” in Fiscal 2023, due to our reliance on partnership model of centres. In Fiscal 2025 our Company reported negative operating cash flow primarily due to an increase in accrued income and trade receivables. Our Company offered Research and Development services to our Singapore-based Holding Company; however, the corresponding cash inflow was pending as of the balance sheet date. This led to higher trade receivables at year-end. Additionally, our Company provides support services to our promoter group entity, Carving Futures, Inc. as part of its international expansion strategy. While these initiatives contributed to revenue growth, the timing difference between revenue recognition and actual collections impacted cash flow from operations. Further, we had negative cash flow from “Investing activities” in Fiscals 2024 and 2025, as our Company recorded a net cash outflow from investing activities, primarily due to establishing more  *“Company Learning Centres”*  . This shift led to increased capital expenditure towards setting up centres. The move was aimed at strengthening operational control and ensuring long-term scalability. Additional investments were made under other financial assets and in bank deposits with maturities exceeding three months.
Any such negative cash flows in the future could adversely affect our business, financial condition and results of operations. For further details, please see “  *Restated Financial Information*  ” and “  *Management’s Discussion and* *Analysis of Financial Condition and Results of Operations*  ” on pages 295 and 359.

* **11.** *    * **Our growth strategy involves opening new centres that may typically take 8- 12 months to achieve breakeven, which** * * **could result in continued negative cash flows.** *
We are currently in a growth phase and intend to expand our network by opening new centres across multiple geographies. We opened 25, 48, 20 and 19 New Centres for the six months period ended September 30, 2025, and Fiscals 2025, 2024, and 2023. Additionally, we closed Nil, 8, 20 and 17 Network Centres for the six months period ended September 30, 2025, and Fiscals 2025, 2024, and 2023. Network Centres were first discontinued in Fiscal 2021 to ensure our operations were consolidated under a more standardized and centrally governed structure. These discontinuations may have resulted in sunk costs, underutilized assets, or operational inefficiencies.  Newly opened centres typically require a ramp-up period of approximately 8- 12 months to achieve operational breakeven. During this period, such centres incur operating expenses without generating sufficient revenue to cover costs, which may contribute to negative cash flows from operations. The timing and extent of breakeven for each centre depend on factors such as location, market demand, operational efficiency, and brand awareness in the region. If the revenue from newly opened centres is lower than expected or their breakeven period is longer than anticipated, our cash requirements will increase and our profitability and liquidity could be adversely affected. Prolonged periods of negative cash flows could limit our ability to fund future expansion, meet operational needs, or invest in other strategic initiatives, which could, in turn, impact our growth prospects.
* **12.** *    * **Our inability to continue recruiting and/or retaining our clinical professionals and qualified personnel at our** * * **centres could impact our business and financial results.** *
We have developed a pan-India network of 136 centres, across 57 cities and 20 states and union territories in India, as on September 30, 2025. We have a multidisciplinary team of clinical professionals, and have engaged child and

50
adolescent psychologists, speech-language pathologists, occupational therapists, special educators and behaviour therapists who provide services at these centres. These clinical professionals possess specialized expertise essential to deliver individualized and effective intervention services. The specialized nature of this sector necessitates a high standard of care, which can only be achieved through continuous investment in recruiting, training, and retaining qualified staff. Further, the success and competitive advantage of our centres depends, in part, on the number and quality of the clinical professionals and other personnel of our facilities and our maintenance of good relations with those clinical professionals. As of September 30, 2025, we employed 81 developmental and clinical psychologists, 136 occupational therapists, 87 speech-language pathologists and 50 special educators. For further details on the clinical professionals our team includes, please see “  *Our Business*  ” on page 217.

While we have engaged over 339  full-time clinical professionals as on September 30, 2025, given the availability of such trained clinical professionals in the market is limited, we cannot assure you that as we expand, our network of clinical professionals will also be able to expand. We also compete with other private and public healthcare providers to attract and retain clinical professionals specifically those skilled to deal with NDDs from a limited pool of candidates. The availability in certain areas of suitably qualified personnel can be further limited in the Tier 3 cities where we operate. To the extent we cannot recruit qualified personnel, we may be required to limit the services provided by these centres, which would have a corresponding adverse effect on our results of operations, or we may be required to recruit clinical professionals from other locations, which would increase our costs as we would have to provide for their relocation.
We have also engaged certain consultants, with whom we have entered into contractual arrangements to ensure service continuity and quality standards. Any disruption in these contractual relationships, whether due to termination, non- renewal, or disputes, may result in service gaps, compromised care delivery, or reputational harm.
Increased competition for skilled clinical professionals or changes in applicable labor regulations may result in higher compensation demands or stricter contractual terms, thereby increasing operational costs. Any of these factors may adversely affect our Company’s ability to scale its operations, maintain service quality, and achieve its business objectives.
In addition to our clinical professionals we have also employed more than 80 clinical operations staff along with extensive headquarters and support staff.  *(Source: CARE Report, on page 63)*  . The loss of the services of such professionals and staff or our inability to recruit or train a sufficient number of experienced clinical professionals or staff or our inability to manage the attrition levels in different employee categories may have an adverse effect on our financial results and business prospects.
As of September 30, 2025, the Company had a total 517 permanent employees and 15 consultants, of which 339 are full-time clinical professionals (including psychologists, therapists, and special educators). For further details, please see  *“Our Business - Our Team”*  on page 242  *.*

* **14.** *    * **Our operations are subject to the compliance of certain applicable statutes. In the event we fail to obtain, maintain** * * **or renew our statutory and regulatory licenses, permits and approvals under the relevant statutes, our business,** * * **cash flows and results of operations may be adversely affected.** *
We are required to obtain and maintain a number of statutory and regulatory permits and approvals under central, state and local government rules in India, generally for carrying out our business and for each of our centres including, without limitation, trade licenses, shops and establishment licenses, registrations under the CERR, employee state insurance registration, employees provident fund registration, and no objection certificates from fire departments. For details of material approvals relating to our business and operations, please see “  *Government and Other Approvals*  ” on page 401. Further, in terms of certain lease agreements entered into with our landlords, the obligation to maintain certain approvals and licenses, including the occupation certificates, fire no-objection certificates, generally rests with the respective landlords for our centres and any failure to obtain such licenses and approvals in a timely manner or at all could result in the disruption of our business operations. For further details on risks arising from lessors not having required approvals in place, please see “  *Risk Factor 4 - Our Registered Office, Corporate Office, 90 out of 136 of our* *centres in India and our newly acquired centres in the United States are in premises not owned by us and we have* *only leasehold rights. Lease amount paid for our centres was ₹22.41 million as on September 30, 2025. Additionally,* *monies from our net issue under capital expenditure object will be utilized towards leased premises rent payment, for* *premises having leased for tenure of 11 months -  3 years. Our landlords may not renew leases of existing centres* *with us or renegotiate terms of our leases, which could adversely affect our business, financial condition and results* *of operations.*  ” on page 41.
Certain of these permits and approvals are valid for a certain period and are required to be renewed at regular intervals in accordance with the timelines prescribed under the relevant statutes or as may be provided under their respective terms. We need to apply for certain such approvals, including the renewal of approvals that expire from time to time, in the ordinary course of our business. To support our expansion plans, we may also consider entering into different jurisdictions wherein we may be required to fulfil the state-wise respective compliances, laws and regulatory norms which differ from state to state. Further, the nation-wide implementation of the CERR Act is under various stages of implementation in the regions where operate our centres. Any such changes in implementation of the CERR Act and allied statutes and rules thereunder, including with respect to applicability to our Facilities, could result in us being deemed to be in contravention of such laws and may require us to apply for additional approvals. We have applied for, but not obtained the CERR registrations for some of our centres in Uttar Pradesh, Sikkim, Telangana, West Bengal, Rajasthan, Bihar and Jharkhand. Additionally, two Shops and Establishments Registrations for one centres in Sikkim, and one centre in Jammu and Kashmir are pending to be received. For further details on pending approvals, please see “  *Government and Other Approvals - Material Approvals pending in respect of our Company*  ” on page 403.

permits, approvals and licenses and we cannot assure you that our landlords will continue to extend cooperation and assistance in a timely manner, or at all. Any failure by our landlords to do so may adversely impact the operations at our centres.
While our services currently fall under the exempt category of healthcare services under the Goods and Services Tax regime and hence we have not obtained GST registrations for our centres, there remains a possibility that the broader healthcare industry may be brought under the GST framework in the future. While we have not any instances of non-compliance in the six months period ended September 30, 2025 and for the Fiscals 2025, 2024 and 2023 which materially affected our operations or our business, if there is any failure by us to comply with the applicable regulations or if the regulations governing our business are amended, we may incur increased costs, be subject to penalties, have our approvals and permits revoked or suffer a disruption in our operations, any of which could adversely affect our business.

* **15.** *    * **If we do not receive payments on time from our clients, our financial condition, cash flows and results of operations** * * **may be materially and adversely affected.** *
We derive our revenue from our clients who may be families availing our intervention services for their children or the entities we export our services to. Clients availing our intervention services may pay either on a per-session basis or through the purchase of therapy plans. This variability in billing structure could create complexities in revenue recognition, cash flow management, and financial forecasting. Our trade receivables have increased in recent periods. As of September 30, 2025, our trade receivables amounted to ₹110.64 million, representing 25.64% of the total revenue from operations. The increase in outstanding receivables reflects a longer collection cycle and may adversely affect our liquidity, cash flows and working capital requirements.
Additionally, if there are any errors or delays in invoicing or collection, or if clients default on payments, our financial condition and results of operations could be adversely affected. There can be no assurance that our billing processes will be consistently accurate or timely, which may result in client dissatisfaction, increased operational costs, and adverse impacts on our reputation and financial performance. Macroeconomic conditions which are beyond our control, such as a potential economic crises, could also result in financial difficulties for our clients. Such conditions could cause our clients to delay payment, request modifications of their payment terms, or default on their payment obligations to us, all of which could increase our receivables. Although trade receivables constituted a lower percentage of our revenue in earlier periods, the recent increase indicates that such receivables may continue to increase as our revenue grows.

## * **8.** *    * **Risks related to the Objects of the Issue** *

While all our centres operate in premises that we lease from third-parties, we incur costs towards setting up new centres which include entering into agreements with third parties such as landlords and vendors, to design and renovate our centres in accordance with our specifications and quality standards and under the time frames that we provide. We also incur costs towards personnel and operations and integrating the internal controls and technology at emerging centres, and a failure to set such sysstems up or integrate them with our wider network could result in the disruption of our ongoing operations, diversion of the attention of our management, and other unforeseen liabilities or costs. Certain risks involved in setting up new centres include the following:

a.  delays in completing renovation works in line with our internal policies and specifications; b.  breach by third parties such as vendors and landlords of their obligations under their agreements with us; c.  delay or failure to secure approvals, permits and licenses or failure to comply with the conditions of such approvals; d.  inability to obtain requisite financing at favourable costs or at all; e.  difficulties arising from coordinating and consolidating corporate and administrative functions, including the integration of internal controls and procedures; f.  recruiting and retaining clinical professionals at our centres; and g.  unforeseen legal, regulatory, contractual, labour or other issues, which could affect the profitability of our centres.
Further, there is no assurance that we will be able to identify suitable sites or targets for expansion or that we will be able to procure favourable terms or that the setting up of centres will be completed in a timely manner, or at all. In the past, we have faced certain delays in the openings of our centres, and while these delays did not have a material

55
impact on our revenue or on the relevant centres’ operations, we cannot assure you that such instances will not occur again. Further, upon completion of emerging centres, we may take time to achieve the operating levels we expect from such emerging centres and thereby fail to achieve our targeted returns on such investments. In addition, competitors may already have established operations in such areas, and we may find it difficult to attract clients in such new areas or may be unsuccessful in managing the risks of such expansions. Any future delays in the setting up, stabilization of operations, relocation or renovation of our centres and any cost overruns in relation thereto could cause us to incur greater expenses and lead to lower growth in our Revenue from Operations from such centres, which could adversely affect our business, financial condition, results of operations and cash flows.

## * **8.** *    * **Risks related to the Objects of the Issue** *

* **17.** *    * **Some of our previously implemented centre business models have been discontinued in the past. As we continue** * * **to expand, we may introduce more such models, which may also be unsuccessful or require to be discontinued,** * * **adversely affecting our business, results of operations and revenues.** *
In the past, we have introduced certain business models for the roll-out of new centres with the objective of enhancing our service offerings, operational efficiency, or client experience. However, not all of these models have met our strategic, financial or operational expectations, and certain such models have subsequently been modified, scaled down, or discontinued entirely. Two of such discontinued models are the joint venture model and the network centre model. Through the joint venture, we had partnered up with another company in August 2024, to run centres under our brand name with our clinical know-how and standard operating procedures, starting with establishing 9 (nine) centres as part of its pilot run. These centres were eventually taken over by our company in March 2025, discontinuing this joint venture. We launched network centres in Fiscal 2019 in partnership with existing therapy centres across India (  *“*   **Network Centres**   *”*  ). Through this model, we provided capacity building, capability enhancement, and brand support to these centres, enabling them to deliver improved services under the ecosystem and branding, while operating as an independently operated centre. While the model offered initial advantages in expanding reach through aligned independent therapy centres, it presented recurring challenges in ensuring consistent adherence to clinical protocols, operational standards, and regulatory compliance frameworks defined by our Company.  *“Network Centres”*  were first discontinued in Fiscal 2021 to ensure our operations were consolidated under a more standardized and centrally governed structure. These discontinuations may have resulted in sunk costs, underutilized assets, or operational inefficiencies.
As part of our growth strategy, we intend to continue exploring and piloting new business models, which may vary in terms of format, services offered, revenue structure, or client engagement mechanisms. While such experimentation is essential for innovation and our competitive positioning, there is no assurance that these new models will achieve commercial success or scalability. Failure of these models may result from factors including, but not limited to, incorrect market assessment, ineffective execution, unanticipated costs, regulatory constraints, or lack of client acceptance. Further, such outcomes may divert management time and financial resources from our core operations and impact overall profitability. Repeated failure of newly introduced models may also adversely affect our brand image, stakeholder confidence, and our ability to attract clients or engage Licensed Professionals to partner up with us for our existing centre models.

Chunk 102
----------------------------------------
# RAYS OF BELIEF LIMITED Corporate Identity Number : U85110DL2017PLC322623  REGISTERED OFFICE CORPORAT[...]

## * **8.** *    * **Risks related to the Objects of the Issue** *

Accordingly, while introduction of new business models remains a key pillar of our strategy, the introduction and potential failure of new centre business models may have a material adverse effect on our business, financial condition, results of operations and prospects.
* **18.** *    * **If we fail to manage our growth or implement our growth strategies (which includes expansion of our centres** * * **through both organic and inorganic growth opportunities, driving cost efficiencies and introducing new service** * * **offerings), our business, financial condition and results of operations may suffer.** *
Our growth depends on, among other things, our ability to establish and manage additional centres though our organic and inorganic expansion strategies, drive cost efficiencies and introduce new offerings and intervention plans to continue building on our image as a holistic care provider. Our growth strategy is also partially dependent upon our ability to identify and successfully complete acquisitions, and other strategic initiatives. For details and the associated risks with the recently completed acquisition of our U.S subsidiaries, please see “  *Risk Factor 19 -We have recently* *acquired Mom’s Belief US, Inc. and Allergy & Immunology, Virginia LLC. to further our strategic objectives, and will* *continue to pursue strategic acquisitions for inorganic growth. Our inability to successfully integrate the operations* *of Mom’s Belief US, Inc. and Allergy & Immunology, Virginia LLC or the operations of any entities that we may* *acquire in the future could adversely impact our business, financial condition, results of operations, cash flows and* *prospects.*  ” on page 56. We intend to continue expanding our operations by opening additional centres in untapped geographies, recruiting high-quality clinical professionals, conducting R&D to adopt new technologies for remote or

56
data-driven care, improving or expanding our service offerings and growing brand awareness and loyalty. For details, please see “  *Our Business -  Our Growth Strategies*  ” on page 230.
We propose to use the Net Proceeds raised from the Issue for establishing centres across India and investment in brand awareness and inclusive outreach programs. The success of such initiatives is, however, subject to various uncertainties, including regulatory delays, challenges in recruiting specialized clinical professionals, logistical constraints, and the evolving needs of the behavioural health domain.
As we expand our business, it is important that we continue to maintain high levels of client service and satisfaction. If we are unable to continue to provide high quality services, our reputation, as well as our business, results of operations and financial condition would be adversely affected.

* **19.** *    * **We have recently acquired Mom’s Belief US, Inc. and Allergy & Immunology, Virginia LLC. to further our** * * **strategic objectives, and will continue to pursue strategic acquisitions for inorganic growth. Our inability to** * * **successfully integrate the operations of Mom’s Belief US, Inc. and Allergy & Immunology, Virginia LLC or the** * * **operations of any entities that we may acquire in the future could adversely impact our business, financial** * * **condition, results of operations, cash flows and prospects.** *
On June 23, 2025, our Company acquired Mom's Belief US, Inc., as our wholly owned Subsidiary and Allergy and Immunology Virginia, LLC, as our Step-Down Subsidiary in the USA, pursuant to which, we added 3 (three) centres, located in the state of Virginia. This acquisition shall help us expand our behavioural health services in the USA, including support for children with NDDs.
Mom’s belief US was incorporated on April 04, 2025. Below is financial summary which is used for preparing pro forma financials for the year ending March 31, 2025:
|  Particulars  |  FY 2024-25  (₹ in millions)  |
|:---|:---|
| Total Income  | 367.23  |
| Total Expenses  | 360.23  |
| Profit Before Tax  | 7.00  |
| Tax Expenses  | Nil  |
| Profit for the Year  | 7.00  |
| Other Comprehensive Income  | 8.84  |
| Total Comprehensive Income for the Year | 15.84  |

For further details on the acquisition and its impact on our financial position, please see  *“Restated Financial* *Information”, “Pro Forma Consolidated Financial Information”, “Summary of Pro Forma Consolidated Financial* *Information”, “Management’s Discussion and Analysis of Financial Condition and Results of Operations - Pro* *Forma Consolidated Financial Information” and*  “  *History and Certain Corporate Matters - Acquisition of Mom’s* *Belief US, Inc. and Allergy & Immunology Virginia, LLC*  ” on pages 295 , 353, 89, 365 and 265, respectively. These centres engage in the allergy, asthma and immunology medical practice. The clinical team includes medical doctors, registered nurses and supporting staff. The practice also caters to a paediatric population who may have overlapping developmental and behavioural needs, a portion of which, could include children with NDDs.
Our strategy is to benefit from this paediatric population who may be in need of our services, and set up our operations as a behavioural therapy care provider in the acquired centres, post which we aim to further expand our footprint in the United States. Considering the difference of services provided by the centres currently and the services we aim to provide, we may be unable to successfully launch our behavioural care services. The overall integration of the business may result in material unanticipated problems, expenses, liabilities, competitive responses and inability to attract clients. As a result, we will be required to devote significant management attention and resources to integrate our business practices and operations with those of the acquired centres in the United States. The integration process may disrupt the businesses and, if implemented ineffectively, would restrict the realization of the full expected benefits. Our failure to meet the challenges involved in integration and to realize the anticipated benefits of the acquisition transaction could cause an interruption of, or a loss of momentum in, the activities of the combined businesses and could adversely affect our business, results of operations, cash flows and financial condition. Further, we have, through this acquisition, assumed the liabilities of our Subsidiaries, which would expose us claims from existing clients of the Subsidiaries, or other third parties and could have adverse effects on our operating results.

57

As part of our strategy, we also intend to set up more centres in countries such as the United Arab Emirates and United Kingdom. Establishing a local presence internationally would facilitate sales of our services, marketing and business development activities and provide us with timely insights into the economic requirements and regulatory environment in such markets. Our ability to acquire new businesses and integrating them into our business is dependent on a number of factors including our ability to identify suitable businesses to acquire in synergy with our existing operations and limitations on our capital resources, amongst others. The acquisition and integration of new businesses like our acquisition of our Subsidiaries in the United States involve a number of risks, including:
a.  inability to integrate or benefit from acquired services in a profitable manner; b.  unanticipated costs or liabilities associated with the acquisition, such as receivables which may be written off or unidentified liabilities; c.  inability to recruit and retain qualified clinical professionals; d.  lower than expected sales of services; e.  problems in integration of the acquired business’s accounting, human resources and other administrative systems, including management information, purchasing, accounting, finance, billing, payroll and benefits and regulatory compliance; f.  inability to identify all the risks, liabilities and challenges in relation to the proposed acquisition; g.  demands placed on our management related to the increase in our overall business size after an acquisition; h.  diversion of management’s attention from existing operations to the integration of the acquired Subsidiaries ; i.  integration of the acquired Subsidiaries’ existing systems into our systems; j.  difficulties in the assimilation and retention of employees of the acquired Subsidiaries; k.  difficulties in the maintenance of relationships with clients and key relationships of an acquired business; l.  incurring significantly higher capital expenditures and operating expenses, which could substantially limit our operating or financial flexibility; m.  entering new and unfamiliar markets; n.  difficulties in coordinating the sales and marketing functions of the acquired business with our existing business; o.  ongoing obligations under agreements related to the acquisition; p.  infringement claims, violation of laws, commercial disputes, tax liabilities and other known and unknown liabilities; q.  inheritance of claims or liabilities, as a result of strategic acquisitions; and r.  unexpected economic or political conditions or changes in the regulatory environment.
Accordingly, we cannot assure you that our current or future acquisitions will prove value accretive to us and to our shareholders. If any of the risks described above (or a combination thereof) or any other incidental risks should materialize, our business, results of operations, financial condition and prospects may be adversely affected. We may not be able to maintain the levels of operating efficiency that acquired companies achieved separately. Successful integration of acquired operations will depend upon our ability to manage those operations and to eliminate redundant and excess costs. We may not be able to achieve the cost savings and other benefits that we would hope to achieve from acquisitions. Failure in effectively acquiring and integrating acquired businesses may result in diminution, loss or write-off of our investments in such businesses, which could materially adversely affect our business, results of operations, cash flows and financial condition.

## * **8.** *    * **Risks related to the Objects of the Issue** *

* **20.** *    * **Low public awareness, limited target age group, and social stigma associated with NDDs may restrict demand for** * * **our services and limit our growth.** *
The Company was founded, primarily to address key barriers related to NDDs in the behavioural health domain, which continues to face challenges arising from low public awareness and the widespread social stigma associated with developmental disabilities. In many regions, particularly semi-urban and rural areas, misconceptions, cultural sensitivities, and lack of reliable information may discourage a potential client from seeking early diagnosis or intervention services for children with NDDs.
Even in cases where a diagnosis is made, families may choose not to avail of our services due to prevailing stigma, thereby reducing our potential client base. Since our services consist of early intervention programs for children up to age 15, this limits our addressable market to a specific age group, and the impact of stigma and low awareness can further reduce uptake. These factors may lead to underutilization of our existing centres, slow the pace of geographical expansion. Such constraints could adversely affect our revenue, growth prospects, and long-term operational sustainability.

58
* **21.** *    * **Low enrolment rates, program discontinuation, and payment constraints may adversely impact our operational** * * **efficiency, revenues, and profitability** *
We prepare intervention plans for children with NDDs which are personalised based on each child’s unique needs and condition severity. The individual performance of each child depends on various factors, including personal ability, family environment and physical health, all of which impacts the success of the plans prepared by us.
Our Company aims to make our programs accessible to all regardless of their means and accordingly allows for variations in our payment terms as per the needs of the family. Health insurance plans do not cover our services, making clients solely responsible for the entire cost of our programs. The growth of client receivables, or deterioration in the ability to collect on these accounts due to the significant financial burden placed on clients, could have an adverse effect on our business, results of operations, and financial condition. If our clients experience dissatisfaction with services, or dispute the pricing of our programmes, we may find it difficult to increase the use of our services within our existing client base and it may be more difficult to attract new clients. While we have a strict refund policy which restricts refunding to only certain services, we may still be required to grant refunds, any of which could negatively impact our operating results and harm our business.

* **22.** *    * **Our “Upskilling Academy” initiative currently does not generate direct revenue from operations and may continue** * * **to operate as a cost center, which could adversely affect our margins and results of operations.** *
We have established Upskilling Academies to provide specialized training and professional development for our clinical and operational staff. For the six months period ended September 30, 2025 and for the Fiscals 2025, 2024, and 2023, these Upskilling Academies have not contributed to our Revenue from Operations. While we believe these academies are essential for maintaining service quality and clinical standards, there can be no assurance that they will generate direct revenue in the future or that the costs associated with maintaining them will be offset by increased operational efficiencies. Any inability to effectively manage the costs of these academies or to realize the intended clinical benefits could impact our financial condition.
* **23.** *    * **None of the directors of our Company possess formal qualifications or specialized expertise directly related to the** * * **specific business activities and industry in which our Company operates.** *
While the members of our Board collectively bring extensive experience in areas such as finance, management, healthcare, law, commerce, administration, and corporate governance, no one have obtained formal training or qualifications (clinically) held positions that are directly aligned with the technical or operational aspects of our business sector. Given the nature of our industry, effective leadership often requires a deep understanding of market dynamics, regulatory frameworks, technological developments, and competitive forces specific to this sector. The absence of directors with such specialized industry background may limit the ability of our Board to anticipate and respond effectively to sector-specific challenges or emerging opportunities. This could, in turn, impact on the strategic decision-making process and operational oversight of our Company.
While our management team is supported by experienced professionals who handle the day-to-day affairs of the business, reliance on external advisors or functional experts for specialized matters may increase operational costs and elongate the decision-making process. Consequently, any inability of the Board to offer industry-specific guidance and insight could adversely affect our Company’s business operations.

## * **24.** *    * **Absence of Listed Industry Peers may affect investor benchmarking and valuation.** *

There are currently no listed companies in India engaged in a business directly comparable to ours, which makes it difficult for investors to benchmark our performance against industry peers  *(Source: CARE Report, on page 65)*  . Our Company is a For-Profit Social Enterprise operating in the behavioural health domain, specifically providing personalised intervention plans for children with Neurodevelopmental Disorders (“  **NDDs**  ”), including Autism Spectrum Disorder (“  **ASD**  ”), Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (“  **ADHD**  ”), Down Syndrome (“  **DS**  ”), Cerebral Palsy (“  **CP**  ”), Intellectual Disability (“  **ID**  ”), Learning Disabilities (“  **LD**  ”), and Global Developmental Delays (“  **GDD**  ”). Our business model is centered on delivering holistic, family-centric solutions, with a focus on accessibility, affordability, and quality of care.
To the best of our knowledge and based on publicly available information, there are currently no listed companies in India or foreign jurisdictions whose business portfolio is comparable with that of our business and comparable to our scale of operations. As a result, investors may find it difficult to evaluate our business, financial performance, and

59
future prospects relative to industry benchmarks. The absence of comparable peers may also impact the valuation of our Company and may affect investor perception of our growth potential. Therefore, investors must rely on their own examination of our accounting ratios, Non-GAAP Measures and key performance indicators relating to our financial and operating performance for the purposes of investment in this Issue. We cannot assure you that our Non-GAAP Measures, key performance indicators and accounting ratios will improve in the future. An inability to improve or maintain our Non-GAAP Measures, key performance indicators and accounting ratios may adversely affect the market price of the Equity Shares.
Further, due to the nascent and evolving nature of the behavioural health and NDDs sector in India, there may be uncertainties with respect to market size, future demand, regulatory developments, and competition from emerging or diversified players entering the space. Any inability on our part to effectively adapt to such changes or to continue to differentiate ourselves in this unique segment could adversely affect our business, financial condition, results of operations, and prospects.
* **25.** *    * **Our Company may be unable to adapt to reforms and developments in the industry which we operate in, and the** * * **uncertainty associated with such reforms could adversely affect the pricing and demand for our products.** *

The NDDs sector, especially in terms of its diagnostic and intervention aspects, is subject to continuous advancements in medical, psychological, and educational methodologies, and the Company’s failure to adapt to such developments in a timely and effective manner may affect the quality and relevance of its services. To ensure we are updated with all these reforms and advancements, we have engaged over 339 full-time clinical professionals with the relevant expertise, and conduct constant R&D. We also publish, on our website, some of the studies we base our programs on so as to help our clients be informed as well.
While we have, since commencement of our operations, had no difficulty in adapting to new reforms,  we cannot assure you that we will continue to have the right resources to do so in the future as well. Any rapid or drastic reform or development which we may have trouble adapting to, could not only make our services less competitive, but could also potentially eliminate or substantially reduce the demand for certain of our services and offerings. These factors may have a material adverse effect on the Company's operations, financial condition, and future prospects.

* **27.** *    * **Our clinical professionals, employees and members of the staff may engage in misconduct or other improper or** * * **illegal activities which could have an adverse effect on our business, results of operations, and financial condition.** *
Our business heavily relies on in-person interactions between the children and our clinical professionals and members of the staff. Due to this nature of our business, we are exposed to the risk of misconduct by our clinical professionals and other employees such as providing inferior or negligent service, engaging in malpractice, violation of laws or regulations, committing fraud, harming the children or mishandling personal healthcare information.
Further, the qualifications and practicing activities of our clinical professionals are strictly regulated by applicable laws, regulations, policies and guidelines, as well as by applicable codes of professional conduct or ethics. Our Company has received complaints from our clients in the past regarding certain aspects of our services or minor cases of misconduct by our clinical professionals, these complaints however, have not been regarding malpractice on part of our clinical professionals, employees and members of the staff, and have been resolved by us and required actions have been taken promptly. We also have standard operating procedures in place for quality control, by way of which we enforce a feedback mechanism. We obtain feedback from clients on a weekly basis and conduct monthly reviews to take appropriate corrective measures. However, despite such ongoing efforts, we cannot entirely eliminate the possibility of similar occurrences in the future. In the event that any further violations or breaches by our clinical professionals and other employees occur, our Company may face criminal penalties, fines, revocation of regulatory approvals, and reputational damage. These consequences could negatively affect our business operations, financial results, and overall standing.

Our Company’s ability to deliver effective and scalable behavioural health domain is increasingly reliant on the use of technology, including digital therapy tools, data-driven progress tracking systems, telehealth platforms, and parent engagement applications. Continued innovation and integration of technology are essential to enhance service delivery, personalize interventions, improve clinical outcomes, and ensure operational efficiency.
However, the field of healthcare technology is rapidly evolving, and user expectations, particularly among digitally aware caregivers, continue to shift. If our Company fails to keep pace with technological advancements, adapt to emerging digital trends, or effectively implement new platforms, it may experience reduced user engagement, inefficiencies in service delivery, or a decline in its competitive edge.

## * **32.** *    * **A cyber-security breach could adversely affect our operations.** *

Our Company is, by the nature of the services provided, privy to sensitive and confidential information including a child’s diagnosis, health and relationship with family members. Further, this data is utilised to help us enhance and improve our services. We are subject to data privacy laws, rules and regulations that regulate the use of client data. Compliance with these laws, rules and regulations may restrict our business activities, require us to incur increased expense and devote considerable time to compliance efforts. The existing and emerging data privacy regulations limit the extent to which we can use personal identifiable information and limit our ability to use third-party firms in connection with students’ data and may hinder in student acquisition.
Our Company has, in 2022, registered a first information report (“  **FIR**  ”) against an ex-employee alleging theft and unauthorized misappropriation of proprietary data belong to our Company as well as criminal breach of trust, cheating and other allied offences. punishable under Section 66B of the Information Technology Act, 2000. For further details, please see “  *Outstanding Litigation and Material Developments*  ” on page 394. Such proceedings could divert the management’s time and attention and consume financial resources. They may also adversely impact our reputation in terms of cyber security.
A number of these laws, rules and regulations are relatively new and their interpretation and application remain uncertain and are also subject to change and may become more restrictive in the future. For instance, the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023 applies to processing of personal data, which has been collected, disclosed, shared or processed within India. Compliance with such data privacy norms may require us to incur significant expenditure, which may adversely impact our financial condition and cash flows. We have implemented role-based access controls, cloud encryption, and two-factor authentication across key systems. Data handling practices are reviewed periodically to align with applicable privacy regulations. However, any failure to maintain the integrity of internal or client data could harm our reputation or subject us to costs, liabilities, fines or lawsuits.

We rely on our information technology systems in connection with enrolments, conducting assessments, content development and distribution, receipt of payment and the general running of our day-to-day business. As our operations grow in size and scope, we must continuously upgrade our systems and infrastructure, while maintaining the reliability and integrity of our systems and infrastructure in a cost-effective manner.

## * **32.** *    * **A cyber-security breach could adversely affect our operations.** *

We have engaged third party service providers for regular maintenance of our information technology infrastructure, such as for maintenance of online portal management and client relationship management system. We also rely on credit card processors and payment gateways to receive payments from clients. Any deficiency of service of such

63
third party service providers may affect our operation and business. In the past, we have experienced down periods when our third-party credit card or payment mobile app processors are unable to process the online payments of our clients, disrupting our ability to receive such payments.
We have entered into agreements with technology providers. Our operations depend on the various software and their configuration, architecture and interconnection specifications, as well as the information stored in these virtual data centres. For further details on the technology we employ in our business, please see “  *Our Business - Our Technology*  ” on page 244.
We have no physical access or control over the working of the software we rely on and we cannot quickly or easily switch our operations to another technology provider. Termination or suspension of our key agreements with our technology vendors would interrupt our business by affecting our ability to access our data and infrastructure. ## * **32.** *    * **A cyber-security breach could adversely affect our operations.** *

Our Company offers its services at its centres, as well as through the digital mode as e-therapy services. During the COVID-19 pandemic and the resulting widespread lockdowns, we had to halt our operations through centres and shift them entirely to the online platform. The connectivity offered by the online platform spread our reach across borders, and we saw enrolments from thirteen countries besides India. We also offered comprehensive online programs and e- therapy sessions for free or on a pay-as-you-like basis, which helped drive this increase in enrolments. However, following the opening up of the lockdowns and resumption of our centre operations, we faced challenges in retaining these enrolments, due to monetary or logistical factors, resulting in a significant drop in the number of enrolments. As of September 30, 2025, over 111 children were actively enrolled through our digital mode.
The table below sets out the contribution of such services to our Revenue from Operations in the six months period ended September 30, 2025 and in Fiscals 2025, 2024 and 2023 highlighting a significant dip in online enrolments post Fiscal 2023:
|  Particulars  |  Six months period  ended September  30, 2025  (Consolidated)  |  Fiscal 2025  (Standalone)  |  Fiscal 2024  (Standalone)  |  Fiscal 2023  (Standalone)  |
|:---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| Revenue from online services (in ₹  mi ll i o n )  |  2.13 |  4.6  |  6.95 |  11.85 |
| Revenue  from  online  services as a  percentage of Revenue from Opera t io n s ( %) |  0.49 |  1.26 |  2.27 |  5.36 |

The table below sets out the mode through which children are served during the six months period ended September 30, 2025 and for the Fiscals 2025, 2024, and 2023:
|  Modes of enrolment  |  September 30, 2025  |  Fiscal 2025  |  Fiscal 2024  |  Fiscal 2023  |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Online  | 185  | 505  | 601  | 892  |
| Offline  | 7,588  | 8,080  | 8,743  | 6,043  |

There is no assurance that we will be able to attract or retain users at levels experienced during the COVID-19 pandemic, or that we will be able to scale this offering profitably. * **Further, our operations may be governed by the laws of foreign jurisdictions and disputes arising from contracts** * * **in such jurisdictions may be subject to the exclusive jurisdiction of foreign courts.** *
Prior to the US Acquisition, our Company’s operations were majorly limited to India. Our recently acquired Subsidiaries Mom’s Belief US, Inc. and Allergy & Immunology, Virginia, LLC. have operations in USA. Consequently, we have acquired, among other things, all contracts entered into by our Subsidiaries that are governed by laws of Delaware and Virginia, USA. Our clients and employees associated with the US Acquisition are also located in these jurisdictions in the USA and we may therefore be required to comply with the local laws, which are not historically familiar with. For instance, we will be subject to various healthcare, labour, controlled substances and environmental related laws.
We are therefore subject to inherent risks associated with international operations which include difficulties in enforcing contractual rights, foreign currency risks, the burdens of complying with foreign laws and potentially adverse tax consequences, including permanent establishment, intellectual property enforcement issues, changes in foreign trade and investment policies and other barriers and potential difficulties in collecting accounts receivable. In addition, the accounting standards, tax laws and other fiscal regulations in the jurisdictions we operate in may be subject to differing interpretations of various governmental ministries, thus creating uncertainty and potentially unexpected results. This acquisition will now require us to comply with U.S. regulatory approvals, such as those from the Committee on Foreign Investment in the United States (“  **CFIUS**  ”), and the possibility of legal or operational disruptions if any compliance issues arise. Additionally, the Subsidiaries’ operations are denominated in U.S. dollars, subjecting us to currency exchange risks that could impact its profitability and our consolidated financial results.
Some of the health care laws applicable to our operations in the USA include but are not limited to those governing participation under and payment for personal care or community care services rendered to beneficiaries of Medicaid, fraud and abuse laws including the Federal Anti-Kickback Statute and the Ethics in Patient Referrals Act, patient confidentiality laws and privacy and security requirements, including the Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act, 1996, as amended, by the Health Information Technology for Economic and Clinical Health Act ("  **HITECH**  ") and its implementing regulations (collectively "  **HIPAA**  ")and other state specific statutes. Non- compliance, which may be caused due to our unfamiliarity with these laws, would expose us to legal and regulatory actions from the US authorities, which could divert management’s attention, affect our operations and reputation, curtailing our opportunity to expand in the specific jurisdiction.

In the past, there have been certain instances statutory and regulatory non-compliances and delays. There have been delays in filing the statutory forms due to operational and technical difficulties faced by the Company, however the same were appropriately filed after payment of late fees.

Further, our Company had adopted a SAR Scheme in the past, which was subsequently rescinded on August 05, 2025, by way of a shareholders resolution. The resolution for adoption of the SAR Scheme was passed as an ordinary resolution, instead of a special resolution as mandated under the SEBI (Share Based Employee Benefits and Sweat Equity) Regulations, 2021, as amended. Our Company has indebtedness of ₹ 39.95 million as on September 30, 2025, consisting of an unsecured loan in the form of inter-corporate borrowings.
As we intend to pursue a strategy of continued investment in our activities, we will incur additional expenditure in the current and future fiscal periods and may incur additional indebtedness in the future. We may also avail loans from banks and NBFCs. Our ability to borrow and the terms of our borrowings will depend on our financial condition, the stability of our cash flows and our capacity to service debt in a rising interest rate environment.
The actual amount and timing of our future capital requirements may also differ from estimates as a result of, among other things, change in business plans due to prevailing economic conditions, unanticipated expenses, new consumption themes or products, and regulatory changes. To the extent our planned expenditure requirements exceed our available resources, we may be required to seek additional debt or equity financing. We may also have difficulty accessing capital markets, which may make it more difficult or expensive to obtain financing in the future. We may not be successful in obtaining additional funds in a timely manner, or on favourable terms or at all. If we do not have access to additional capital, we may be required to delay, postpone or abandon or reduce capital expenditures and the size of our operations, any of which may adversely affect our business, financial conditions and results of operations.
Our ability to service any indebtedness will depend on our future performance and our ability to generate cash, which, to a certain extent, is subject to general economic, financial, competitive, legislative, legal, regulatory and other factors, many of which are beyond our control. If our future cash flows from operations and other capital resources are insufficient to pay our debt obligations, our contractual obligations, or to fund our other liquidity needs, we may be forced to sell our assets or attempt to restructure or refinance our existing indebtedness. Our ability to restructure or refinance our debt will depend on the condition of the capital markets and our financial condition at such time. Any refinancing of our debt could be at higher interest rates and may require us to comply with more onerous covenants, which could further restrict our business operations. The terms of existing or future debt instruments may restrict us from adopting some of these alternatives.

* **41.** *    * **We rely on third-party contractors for the renovation and general setting up of our centres. Any lapse by such** * * **third-party service contractors may have adverse consequences on our business, reputation, results of operations** * * **and cash flows.** *

We rely on third party contractors for the renovation of our centres. We do not have direct control over these third- party service providers, and there is no guarantee that they will provide satisfactory services to us by completing the project within the stipulated time. These contractors may experience disruptions in their operations or services due to factors beyond their and our control. If any of these contractors’ operations or services are disrupted or terminated, we may not be able to find alternative quality service providers on commercial terms to our satisfaction in a timely and reliable manner, or at all. Due to our centres mainly hosting children with NDDs, we have to ensure that our centres are safe for them and have accordingly prepared strict guidelines for our centre designs. Poor quality service or lapses in service from our third-party service providers may expose us to delays in opening new centres that may adversely affect our business, results of operations and cash flows. We do not enter into any employment agreements with the service personnel of our third-party service providers. However, in the event that any of our third-party service providers default on their employer obligations, we may be held responsible for providing statutory benefits, including the salaries/wages of these employees, or be subject to fines and penalties imposed by regulatory authorities, which may increase our operating expense and adversely affect our business, results of operations and cash flows. While we have not faced any such instances of lapse in services by third party contractors during the past three Fiscals, we cannot assure you that we will not encounter any such lapses in future. Any such instances may adversely affect our business, reputation, results of operations and cash flows.

Our business and results of operations may get adversely affected by factors beyond our control such as acts of God, natural calamities, epidemics, pandemics (manmade and/or natural), flood, orders or restrictions, war or warlike conditions, hostilities, sanctions, blockades, embargoes, detentions, revolutions, riots, looting, strikes, earthquakes, fires or accidents, civil commotion, strike, failure of communication or banking systems, which may require the evacuation of personnel, suspension or curtailment of our centre operations. * **45.** *    * **The impact of outbreaks of diseases on our business and operations is uncertain, and may have an adverse effect** * * **on our business, operations and our future financial performance.** *
As a provider of services that require clients to come to the centres for availing of therapy programs, we are significantly exposed to the public health and economic effects of events such as the COVID-19 pandemic. The impact of pandemics or public health emergencies on our business, operations and financial performance may include, but is not limited to:
a.  adverse impact on the cash flows and access to funds for our potential and existing clients, which may affect their demand for our offerings and adversely affect our Revenue from Operations; b.  restrictions on movement of people and necessary materials required for our business operations as well as risk of our employees contracting the disease; c.  temporary or permanent refusal of employees to return to work and their failure to return our equipment; technology and equipment shortages due to global supply chain issues; and d.  increased costs to ensure the safety of our workforce and continuity of operations while conforming to the measures implemented by various governments.
We have, during the COVID-19 pandemic and may in the event of such similar outbreaks, have to restrict conducting our sessions online. Due to this, we may have to charge a reduced fee for our plans, while our outgoing payments may remain the same, in terms of rent and payments to Licensed Professionals and other clinical professionals employed by us. ## * **49.** *    * **The requirements of being a listed company may strain our resources.** *

We are not a listed company and have not historically been subject to scrutiny of our affairs by shareholders, regulators and the public at large that is associated with being a listed company. As a listed company, we will incur significant legal, accounting, corporate governance and other expenses that we did not incur as an unlisted company. We will be subject to the provisions of the Listing Regulations and the listing agreements to be executed with the Stock Exchanges, which will require us to file audited annual and unaudited quarterly reports with respect to our business and financial condition. If we experience any delays, we may fail to satisfy our reporting obligations and/or we may not be able to readily determine and accordingly report any changes in our results of operations as promptly as other listed companies. Further, as a listed company, we will need to maintain and improve the effectiveness of our disclosure controls and procedures and internal control over financial reporting, including keeping adequate records of daily transactions to support the existence of effective disclosure controls and procedures and internal control over financial reporting. In order to maintain and improve the effectiveness of our disclosure controls and procedures and internal control over financial reporting, significant resources and management attention will be required. As a result, our management’s attention may be diverted from other business concerns, which may adversely affect our business, results of operations and prospects. In addition, we may need to hire additional legal and accounting staff with appropriate experience and technical accounting knowledge, however, we cannot assure you that we will be able to do so in a timely and efficient manner.

We have not paid any dividends in the last three Fiscals. Our ability to pay future dividends will depend on our earnings, financial condition and capital requirements. There can be no assurance that we will generate sufficient income to cover the operating expenses and pay dividends to the shareholders. The declaration and payment of dividends will be recommended by our Board of Directors and approved by our Shareholders, at their discretion, subject to the provisions of the Articles of Association and applicable law, including the Companies Act, 2013. We may retain all future earnings, if any, for use in the operations and expansion of the business. As a result, we may not declare dividends in the foreseeable future. Any future determination as to the declaration and payment of dividends will be at the discretion of our Board and will depend on factors that our Board deems relevant, including among others, our future earnings, financial condition, cash requirements, business prospects and any other financing arrangements. Accordingly, realization of a gain on Shareholders’ investments will depend on the appreciation of the price of the Equity Shares. There is no guarantee that our Equity Shares will appreciate in value.

We periodically test and update our internal processes and systems and there have been no past material instances of failure to maintain effective internal controls and compliance systems. Any lapses in judgment or failures that result from human error can affect the accuracy of our financial reporting. We cannot assure you that deficiencies in our internal controls will not arise in the future, or that we will be able to implement, and continue to maintain, adequate measures to rectify or mitigate any such deficiencies in our internal controls. Any inability on our part to adequately detect, rectify or mitigate any such deficiencies in our internal controls may adversely affect our ability to accurately report, or successfully manage, our financial risks, and to avoid fraud.

* **56.** *    * **Political, economic or other factors that are beyond our control may have an adverse effect on our business and** * * **results of operations.** *
The Indian economy and capital markets are influenced by economic, political and market conditions in India and globally. Our Company is incorporated in India, and all of our assets and employees are located in India. As a result, we are dependent on prevailing economic conditions in India and our results of operations are affected by factors influencing the Indian economy. Further, the following external risks may have an adverse impact on our business and results of operations, should any of them materialize:
a.  increase in interest rates may adversely affect our access to capital and increase our borrowing costs, which may constrain our ability to grow our business and operate profitably;
b.  downgrade of India’s sovereign debt rating by an independent agency or decline in India’s foreign exchange reserves;
c.  political instability, resulting from a change in governmental or economic and fiscal policies, may adversely affect economic conditions in India;
d.  instability in the financial markets and volatility in, and actual or perceived trends in trading activity on, India’s principal stock exchanges;
e.  civil unrest, acts of violence, terrorist attacks, regional conflicts or situations or war, including instability in other countries and adverse changes in geopolitical situations;
f.  India has experienced epidemics and natural calamities such as earthquakes, tsunamis, floods, and drought in recent years;
g.  financial instability and turmoil in other countries; and
h.  contagious diseases such as the COVID-19 pandemic, the highly pathogenic H7N9, H5N1 and H1N1 strains of influenza in birds and swine. A worsening of the current COVID-19 pandemic or any similar future outbreaks of COVID-19, avian or swine influenza or a similar contagious disease could adversely affect the Indian economy and economic activity in the region.
Any slowdown or perceived slowdown in the Indian economy, or in specific sectors of the Indian economy, could adversely affect our business, results of operations, cash flows and financial condition and the price of the Equity Shares. Our performance and the growth of our business depend on the overall performance of the Indian economy as well as the economies of the regional markets in which we operate.

Further, certain actions must be completed prior to the commencement of listing and trading of the Equity Shares such as the Investor’s book entry or ‘demat’ accounts with the depository participants in India, the Allotment of Equity Shares in the Issue and the credit of such Equity Shares to the applicant’s demat account with the depository participant. * **59.** *    * **Pursuant to listing of the Equity shares, we may be subject to pre-emptive surveillance measures like Additional** * * **Surveillance Measures (ASM) and Graded Surveillance Measures (GSM) by the Stock Exchanges in the order to** * * **enhance market integrity and safeguard the interest of the investors.** *
On and post the listing of equity shares, we may be subject to ASM and GSM by the Stock Exchange(s) and the SEBI. These measures have been introduced in order to enhance market integrity and safeguard the interest of investors and to alert and advise investors to be extra cautious and carry out necessary due diligence while dealing in such securities.
The criteria for shortlisting any scrip trading on the Stock Exchange(s) under the ASM is based on an objective criterion as jointly decided by SEBI and the Stock Exchange(s) which include market based dynamic parameters such as high low variations, client concentration, close to close price variation, market capitalization, volume variation, delivery percentage, number of unique PAN’s and price to equity ratio. A scrip is typically subjected GSM measures where there is an abnormal price rise that is not commensurate with the financial heath and fundamentals of a company which inter alia includes factors like earnings, book value, fixed assets and net worth to the equity ratio etc. The price of our equity shares may also fluctuate after the Issue due to several factors such as volatility in the Indian and global securities market, our profitability and performance, the performance of our competitors, change in the estimates of our performance or any other political or economic factor. The occurrence of any of the above-mentioned factors may trigger the parameters identified by SEBI and the Stock Exchange(s) for the placing securities under the GSM and ASM framework. In the event of our Equity Shares are covered under such pre-emptive surveillance measures implemented by SEBI and the Stock Exchange(s), we may be subject to certain additional restrictions in the relation to trading of our Equity Shares such as limiting trading frequency (for example trading either allowed in a week or a month) higher margin requirements of settlement on a trade for trade basis without netting off requirement of settlement on gross basis or freezing price on upper side of trading which may have an adverse effect on the market price of our Equity Shares or may in general cause disruptions in the development of an active market for and trading and liquidity of our Equity Shares and on the reputation and conditions of our Company.

* **61.** *    * **The Issue Price of the Equity Shares may not be indicative of the market price of the Equity Shares after the Issue.** *
On listing, the Equity Shares will be quoted in Indian Rupees on the Stock Exchanges. Any dividends in respect of the Equity Shares will be paid in Indian Rupees and subsequently converted into appropriate foreign currency for repatriation, if required. Any adverse movement in currency exchange rates during the time it takes to undertake such conversion may reduce the net dividend to investors. In addition, any adverse movement in exchange rates during a delay in repatriating the proceeds from a sale of Equity Shares outside India, for example, because of a delay in regulatory approvals that may be required for the sale of Equity Shares, may reduce the net proceeds received by shareholders. For example, the exchange rate between the Indian Rupee and the U.S. dollar has fluctuated in recent years and may continue to fluctuate substantially in the future, which may have an adverse effect on the returns on our Equity Shares, independent of our operating results.
* **62.** *    * **Investors may be subject to Indian taxes arising out of capital gains on the sale of the Equity Shares.** *
Under current Indian tax laws, unless specifically exempted, capital gains arising from the sale of equity shares in an Indian company is generally taxable in India. A securities transaction tax (“  **STT**  ”) is levied on and collected by an Indian stock exchange on which equity shares are sold. Any gain realized on the sale of listed equity shares held for more than 12 months may be subject to long term capital gains tax in India at the specified rates depending on certain factors, such as STT is paid, the quantum of gains and any available treaty exemptions. Accordingly, you may be subject to payment of long term capital gains tax in India, in addition to payment of STT, on the sale of any Equity Shares held for more than 12 months. Furthermore, any gain realized on the sale of listed equity shares held for a period of 12 months or less will be subject to short term capital gains tax in India. Earlier, distribution of dividends by a domestic company was subject to Dividend Distribution Tax (“  **DDT**  ”), in the hands of the company and such dividends were generally exempt from tax in the hands of the shareholders. However, the government of India has amended the Income Tax Act to abolish the DDT regime. Under the extant provisions, any dividend distributed by a domestic company is subject to tax in the hands of the concerned shareholder at the applicable rates. Additionally, the company distributing dividends is required to withhold tax on such payments at the applicable rate. However, non-resident shareholders may claim benefit of the applicable tax treaty, subject to satisfaction of certain conditions.

----------------------------------------

## * **49.** *    * **The requirements of being a listed company may strain our resources.** *

#### **EXTERNAL RISKS**

Furthermore, if non-resident shareholders of entities holding the Equity Shares exit by way of sale or redemption of the shares held by them abroad in such entities, such non-resident shareholders could be taxed on capital gains in India if the offshore shares derive substantial value from Indian assets, subject to certain exemptions. Capital gains arising from the sale of the Equity Shares will be exempt from taxation in India only in limited situations and generally, Indian tax treaties do not limit India’s ability to impose tax on capital gains. As a result, residents of other countries may be liable for tax in India as well as in their own jurisdiction on a gain upon the sale of the Equity Shares. Similarly, any business income realized from the transfer of Equity Shares held as trading assets is taxable at the applicable tax rates subject to any treaty relief, if applicable, to a non-resident seller.
Furthermore, the Finance Act, 2019 amended the Indian Stamp Act, 1899 with effect from July 1, 2020 clarified that, in the absence of a specific provision under an agreement, the liability to pay stamp duty in case of sale of securities through stock exchanges will be on the buyer, while in other cases of transfer for consideration through a depository, the onus will be on the transferor. The stamp duty for transfer of securities other than debentures on a delivery basis is specified at 0.015% and on a non-delivery basis is specified at 0.003% of the consideration amount.

Our Company cannot predict whether any tax laws or other regulations impacting it will be enacted or predict the nature and impact of any such laws or regulations or whether, if at all, any laws or regulations would have a material adverse effect on our Company’s business, results of operations, financial condition and cash flows. * **63.** *    * **QIBs and Non-Institutional Investors are not permitted to withdraw or lower their Bids (in terms of quantity of** * * **Equity Shares or the Bid Amount) at any stage after submitting a Bid, and Retail Individual Bidders are not** * * **permitted to withdraw their Bids after the Bid/Issue Closing Date.** *
Pursuant to the SEBI ICDR Regulations, QIBs and Non-Institutional Investors are required to pay the bid amount on submission of the bid and are not permitted to withdraw or lower their Bids (in terms of quantity of Equity Shares or the Bid Amount) at any stage after submitting a Bid. RIIs can revise or withdraw their Bids during the Bid/Issue Period and until the Bid/Issue Closing Date, but not thereafter. While our Company is required to complete Allotment pursuant to the Issue within such period as may be prescribed under applicable law, events affecting the Bidders’ decision to invest in our Equity Shares, including adverse changes in international or national monetary policy, financial, political or economic conditions, our business, financial condition and results of operations may arise between the date of submission of the Bid and Allotment. Our Company may complete the Allotment of our Equity Shares even if such events occur, and such events limit the Bidders’ ability to sell our Equity Shares allotted pursuant to the Issue or cause the trading price of our Equity Shares to decline on listing. QIBs and Non-Institutional Bidders will not be able to withdraw or lower their bids following adverse developments in international or national monetary policy, financial, political or economic conditions, our business, results of operations, cash flows or otherwise, between the dates of submission of their Bids and Allotment.

* **64.** *    * **The determination of the Price Band is based on various factors and assumptions and the Issue Price of our Equity** * * **Shares may not be indicative of the market price of our Equity Shares after the Issue.** *
The determination of Price Band is based on various factors and assumptions and will be determined by our Company in consultation with the Book Running Lead Manager. Furthermore, the Issue Price of the Equity Shares will be determined by our Company, in consultation with the Book Running Lead Manager through the book building process prescribed under the SEBI ICDR Regulations.
The Issue Price will be based on numerous factors, as described under  *“Basis for Issue Price”*  on page 146 may not be indicative of the market price for our Equity Shares after the Issue. The market price of our Equity Shares could be subject to significant fluctuations after the Issue and may decline below the Issue Price. In addition, the stock market often experiences price and volume fluctuations that are unrelated or disproportionate to the operating performance of a particular company. These broad market fluctuations and industry factors may materially reduce the market price of the Equity Shares, regardless of our Company’s performance. As a result of these factors, we cannot assure you that investors will be able to resell their Equity Shares at or above the Issue Price
